speaker: interviewer
text: In Johannesburg, South Africa, October 16, 1995. Tape 6. Reuben, can you now tell me the names of the places that you went to after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being a short while in [INAUDIBLE], which are a group recuperated from malnutrition, myself, my father, and my uncle, with the permission of the American authorities, left this place. And we decided to go home to Lithuania. The Americans ask if anybody else wants to go. They'll put a transport to take us to the Russian border. We came to the Russian border. The able young men, the Russian army took them to the Russian army. And the weaker one and the younger one, like children that was only 15 years old, they let us through. And we went-- we marched to the railway station, and we hitchhiked trains, which were running quite frequently with military staff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the Russian side already. And the train used to stop at night. It used to go. Anyway, it took us a week to come to Berlin. In Berlin, we arrived two days before the Allied forces. I mean, the Americans, British, and French got permission to enter Berlin, according to Potsdam agreement. And we find there is a Jewish hospital and a Jewish agency pre-war, which is still in the Jewish hands. We were allocated a room for my father, my uncle, and myself. And they had double beds, bunks. And they provided blankets, food, and every amenity what a person needs. And this was a center-- transit center-- for people who came from anywhere. There, I met a young [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] child, maybe 13, with her granny, I think. I don't remember if it was her mother. And they couldn't get organized. They were German Jews from somewhere. They were also in transit.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got organized. I got food from the American army's kitchens in [GERMAN]. The British didn't have much food. The French had nothing. And I used to help this old lady with her niece-- her niece. Her niece? Yes. Because she must have been 12, and this woman must have been 50, 55. It can't be her daughter. Anyway, I used to look after them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Berlin for six, seven months, and then the Allied forces start to-- to divide the city into four parts. For my time, I still could go around between any sector I wanted to. And I met people, and they told us, the Jewish women were liberated by the Russians from Lithuania, and they're somewhere in Poland. My father and my uncle decided to go look for-- for our family. And if they don't come back, if they don't come back to Berlin in two weeks or three weeks' time, I must go to Lodz. My uncle came back in two weeks' time. He said he lost my father on the-- on the road somewhere. They didn't-- they didn't meet together. And if he doesn't come back in two weeks' time, [INAUDIBLE] in Lodz, and then we'll go home. I had a friend, a Polish Jewish guy, in Berlin, and we used to go to cinema every day for free. And one day, I say to him, I don't feel like going. Maybe my father will come. Maybe my mother will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went, and I was sitting on the window, up on the third story window, sitting in the afternoon. And all of a sudden, a big Russian truck stopped. I looked through the window. It is a Russian soldier and two women on top on the truck and a lot of stuff-- bags and suitcases and boxes. And the door opens, and my father jumps out. And a Russian officer and the driver goes out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I run down the steps. And I see it's my father came back, and my Auntie Paula, and my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I realized that my mother is gone. I lost speech. I couldn't speak for three months maybe. The American army gave me therapy for my speech. But those Russian people helped us a lot. They came with food stuff to buy clothing on the black market in Berlin. And they've been to our place twice. We made a few dollars from them. And they were happy, and we were happy. Then, things changed in Berlin. I didn't know what life-- life is. I had the argument with my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was too young to know what is life is, because I only knew one life-- to survive. What is life about? I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had argument with my father, and we parted. And my sister, Shoshanna, and my Auntie Paula joined me. And after a few months, we left Berlin, and we went to the English sector of [? Brunswick ?] and then Frankfurt and Munich-- Munchen. In Munich, they got temporary accommodation. I was looking for a future. I find out there was some DP children's home schools. I went to a few of them I didn't like. One I went by train to [GERMAN]. It's a monastery for nuns, which was converted for refugee children, not only Jewish children. And I came there, and I was welcomed with both hands. I was registered. I was given a bed, given food, given clothing. And when first certificates came to go to Palestine, I went to Palestine. And I've been in Palestine, Israel. I was in the Israeli army. I was under siege for eight months in Israel. I survived. I was wounded. And then my sister came to Israel in 1948. She found me in a-- in a recuperation center. And when my sister got married to a Rhodesian chap-- let him rest in peace-- she took me up two years later. And I went to northern Rhodesia. I got married. I got four beautiful sons, and I got a wife. In '65, '64, I had to have a brain operation from-- from good life in Germany-- fear, dirt, and sickness-- I had cerebral hemorrhage in my brain. I survived. If this would happen a few years earlier, they could not operate in Johannesburg. It took some doing, but with God helps and with the help of the free world, I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] Thank you. When did you come to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: South Africa? To Rhodesia, I came in 1955 from Israel. I've been in northern Rhodesia until 1965. I came here November '65. And I'm still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, do you have some message that you want to give about Holocaust, about survival, about whatever?

speaker: interviewee
text: People ask me, Reuben, why didn't the Jewish nation [? raise ?] against the Germans? I said, well, I say don't tell me they didn't [? raise ?] against the Germans. They did [? raise ?] in Warsaw ghetto. But there were so few left, so desperately, and the German army comparison with today's army-- in those days, it was the strongest army in the world. How can you resist such a army?

speaker: interviewer
text: For every little thing, there are different departments. They had a department to make experiments on women, to make experiments a woman-- they implant, [? outplant, ?] anything. They took children. They-- they-- they cut up pieces. They cut up bodies. They made anything from human beings. How can you resist against such tyranny, such [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] dark ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unbelievable-- unheard of in such a civilized nation. The message is try to survive. Live and let live. And god bless the free world. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I do? [CRYING] All right? [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reuben, who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my late father, which was a survivor from the Holocaust. He died in Minneapolis, Minnesota, USA-- accident at work. On the right-hand of my-- on the right-hand of the picture is my late brother, Chaim, which was liquidated at Dachau concentration camp in Germany. The two little people there on the-- on the right-hand of my father, that's me. And I must have been then-- this must have been in 1938 in Kovno, Lithuania. And this is my sister, Shoshanna, which is also Holocaust survivor. Bless her. And she is in Johannesburg at present, living in Johannesburg. And I-- I still had another sister, Miriam. She was liquidated in Kovno Lithuania by the-- by the Germans in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] . And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my late mother, Zelda Drehspul, let her rest in peace. Died two days after the liberation by the Russian army in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] your machine a long ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, I was young one day. This was a few weeks or months after the liberation by the American troops in Germany. And I was 15 years old. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] God bless America. [CRYING] That's it. You're going to [INAUDIBLE]. My Dachau concentration camp number was, as you can see, 82247. And don't think I wore it with pride. My days were numbered, but I survived the Germans. This is a replica, which I made in Munich after the liberation by the American armies. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Reuben Drehspul, taken in 1946 in [INAUDIBLE], which is a-- was a DP children's center, Team 182. This was before my departure for Israel-- for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were on the far right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm on the far right. No pointer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the children's home from UNRRA Team 182 in [INAUDIBLE], Bavaria, Germany. I'd been housed there, fed there, and accommodated there, and where the nuns looking after us with the American team. From there, we left for Palestine in 1946. I'd been there for eight months or six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This card is the reverse card of [INAUDIBLE], which was presented to me for my departure for Israel. And this is from the same team, UNRRA Team 181. I think so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this signed by Ms. Davis in April 1946. I'm ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me, Reuben Drehspul, a soldier in the Israeli army, which was wounded. And at present to the picture, I'm recuperating from my wounds. We had lady soldiers, girl soldiers, any soldiers, as long as we are Jewish and happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you, Reuben?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the right corner front-- right front.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, a set of currency, which has been printed by the Germans, for the Theresienstadt ghetto. This ghetto was a show piece for the outside world. All sorts of delegations from the free world came to see the ghetto. With this currency, you could have bought anything and everything in this ghetto. It's written in German. And if-- it's written very close. If you falsify it, it's severe punishment. It's got a picture of Moses and the Ten Commandments.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this money, I got it in Berlin from my young girlfriend. I was 15, and she was 12. They were German Jewish. She was-- she was with her mother, or I think her granny. I'm not sure. They were German Jewish. They couldn't survive without my help, or help from somebody else, because they were lonely two people. I organized for them food, maybe some currencies, maybe some medications. And this was a go-away present to me from these two beautiful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slowly. Excuse me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. How did you know I was feeling like that? Thank you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I know something else, too. I'm ready. You can introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left is my wife, [INAUDIBLE]. And Michael is on my left, and David is on my right. And one son I got in United States of America is Ivan.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Hilton, and his wife, Michelle Drehspul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anyone have a message? Does anyone want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to add that many years later, indirectly or directly, that it has affected our family in a great way and possibly, many other families as well throughout the world.
